Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## FOURIER NEURAL OPERATOR FOR PARAMETRIC PARTIAL DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS

**Zongyi Li** zongyili@caltech.edu

**Nikola Kovachki** nkovachki@caltech.edu

**Kamyar Azizzadenesheli** kamyar@purdue.edu
**Burigede Liu Kaushik Bhattacharya Andrew Stuart** bgl@caltech.edu bhatta@caltech.edu astuart@caltech.edu

**Anima Anandkumar** anima@caltech.edu

## ABSTRACT

The classical development of neural networks has primarily focused on learning mappings between finite-dimensional Euclidean spaces. Recently, this has been generalized to neural operators that learn mappings between function spaces. For partial differential equations (PDEs), neural operators directly learn the mapping from any functional parametric dependence to the solution. Thus, they learn an entire family of PDEs, in contrast to classical methods which solve one instance of the equation. In this work, we formulate a new neural operator by parameterizing the integral kernel directly in Fourier space, allowing for an expressive and efficient architecture. We perform experiments on Burgers’ equation, Darcy flow, and Navier-Stokes equation. The Fourier neural operator is the first ML-based method to successfully model turbulent flows with zero-shot super-resolution. It is up to three orders of magnitude faster compared to traditional PDE solvers. Additionally, it achieves superior accuracy compared to previous learning-based solvers

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Many problems in science and engineering involve solving complex partial differential equation (PDE) systems repeatedly for different values of some parameters. Examples arise in molecular dynamics, micro-mechanics, and turbulent flows. Often such systems require fine discretization in order to capture the phenomenon being modeled. As a consequence, traditional numerical solvers are slow and sometimes inefficient. For example, when designing materials such as airfoils, one needs to solve the associated inverse problem where thousands of evaluations of the forward model are needed. A fast method can make such problems feasible.

**Conventional solvers vs. Data-driven methods.** Traditional solvers such as finite element methods (FEM) and finite difference methods (FDM) solve the equation by discretizing the space. Therefore, they impose a trade-off on the resolution: coarse grids are fast but less accurate; fine grids are accurate but slow. Complex PDE systems, as described above, usually require a very fine discretization, and therefore very challenging and time-consuming for traditional solvers. On the other hand, data-driven methods can directly learn the trajectory of the family of equations from the data. As a result, the learning-based method can be orders of magnitude faster than the conventional solvers.

Machine learning methods may hold the key to revolutionizing scientific disciplines by providing fast solvers that approximate or enhance traditional ones (Raissi et al., 2019; Jiang et al., 2020; Greenfeld et al., 2019; Kochkov et al., 2021). However, classical neural networks map between finite-dimensional spaces and can therefore only learn solutions tied to a specific discretization. This is often a limitation for practical applications and therefore the development of mesh-invariant neural networks is required. We first outline two mainstream neural network-based approaches for PDEs – the finite-dimensional operators and Neural-FEM.
**Finite-dimensional operators.** These approaches parameterize the solution operator as a deep convolutional neural network between finite-dimensional Euclidean spaces Guo et al. (2016); Zhu

& Zabaras (2018); Adler & Oktem (2017); Bhatnagar et al. (2019); Khoo et al. (2017). Such approaches are, by definition, mesh-dependent and will need modifications and tuning for different resolutions and discretizations in order to achieve consistent error (if at all possible). Furthermore, these approaches are limited to the discretization size and geometry of the training data and hence, it is not possible to query solutions at new points in the domain. In contrast, we show, for our method, both invariance of the error to grid resolution, and the ability to transfer the solution between meshes.

**Neural-FEM.** The second approach directly parameterizes the solution function as a neural network (E & Yu, 2018; Raissi et al., 2019; Bar & Sochen, 2019; Smith et al., 2020; Pan & Duraisamy, 2020). This approach is designed to model one specific instance of the PDE, not the solution operator. It is mesh-independent and accurate, but for any given new instance of the functional parameter/coefficient, it requires training a new neural network. The approach closely resembles classical methods such as finite elements, replacing the linear span of a finite set of local basis functions with the space of neural networks. The Neural-FEM approach suffers from the same computational issue as classical methods: the optimization problem needs to be solved for every new instance. Furthermore, the approach is limited to a setting in which the underlying PDE is known.

**Neural Operators.** Recently, a new line of work proposed learning mesh-free, infinitedimensional operators with neural networks (Lu et al., 2019; Bhattacharya et al., 2020; Nelsen & Stuart, 2020; Li et al., 2020b;a; Patel et al., 2021). The neural operator remedies the mesh-dependent nature of the finite-dimensional operator methods discussed above by producing a single set of network parameters that may be used with different discretizations. It has the ability to transfer solutions between meshes. Furthermore, the neural operator needs to be trained only once. Obtaining a solution for a new instance of the parameter requires only a forward pass of the network, alleviating the major computational issues incurred in Neural-FEM methods. Lastly, the neural operator requires no knowledge of the underlying PDE, only data. Thus far, neural operators have not yielded efficient numerical algorithms that can parallel the success of convolutional or recurrent neural networks in the finite-dimensional setting due to the cost of evaluating integral operators. Through the fast Fourier transform, our work alleviates this issue.

**Fourier Transform.** The Fourier transform is frequently used in spectral methods for solving differential equations, since differentiation is equivalent to multiplication in the Fourier domain. Fourier transforms have also played an important role in the development of deep learning. In theory, they appear in the proof of the universal approximation theorem (Hornik et al., 1989) and, empirically, they have been used to speed up convolutional neural networks (Mathieu et al., 2013). Neural network architectures involving the Fourier transform or the use of sinusoidal activation functions have also been proposed and studied (Bengio et al., 2007; Mingo et al., 2004; Sitzmann et al., 2020). Recently, some spectral methods for PDEs have been extended to neural networks (Fan et al., 2019a;b; Kashinath et al., 2020). We build on these works by proposing a neural operator architecture defined directly in Fourier space with quasi-linear time complexity and state-of-the-art approximation capabilities.

2

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

**Our Contributions.** We introduce the Fourier neural operator, a novel deep learning architecture able to learn mappings between infinite-dimensional spaces of functions; the integral operator is restricted to a convolution, and instantiated through a linear transformation in the Fourier domain.

- The Fourier neural operator is the first work that learns the resolution-invariant solution operator for the family of Navier-Stokes equation in the turbulent regime, where previous graph-based neural operators do not converge.

- By construction, the method shares the same learned network parameters irrespective of the discretization used on the input and output spaces. It can do zero-shot super-resolution: trained on a lower resolution directly evaluated on a higher resolution, as shown in Figure 1.

- The proposed method consistently outperforms all existing deep learning methods even when fixing the resolution to be 64 _×_ 64. It achieves error rates that are 30% lower on Burgers’ Equation, 60% lower on Darcy Flow, and 30% lower on Navier Stokes (turbulent regime with Reynolds number 10000). When learning the mapping for the entire time series, the method achieves _<_ 1% error with Reynolds number 1000 and 8% error with Reynolds number 10000.

- On a 256 _×_ 256 grid, the Fourier neural operator has an inference time of only 0 _._ 005s compared to the 2 _._ 2 _s_ of the pseudo-spectral method used to solve Navier-Stokes. Despite its tremendous speed advantage, the method does not suffer from accuracy degradation when used in downstream applications such as solving the Bayesian inverse problem, as shown in Figure 6.

We observed that the proposed framework can approximate complex operators raising in PDEs that are highly non-linear, with high frequency modes and slow energy decay. The power of neural operators comes from combining linear, global integral operators (via the Fourier transform) and nonlinear, local activation functions. Similar to the way standard neural networks approximate highly non-linear functions by combining linear multiplications with non-linear activations, the proposed neural operators can approximate highly non-linear operators.

We have experimented with linear as well as neural network parameterizations of _Rφ_ . We find that the linear parameterization has a similar performance to the previously described direct parameterization, while neural networks have worse performance. This is likely due to the discrete structure of the space Z _[d]_ . Our experiments in this work focus on the direct parameterization presented above.

**Invariance to discretization.** The Fourier layers are discretization-invariant because they can learn from and evaluate functions which are discretized in an arbitrary way. Since parameters are learned directly in Fourier space, resolving the functions in physical space simply amounts to projecting on the basis _e_[2] _[πi][⟨][x,k][⟩]_ which are well-defined everywhere on R _[d]_ . This allows us to achieve zero-shot super-resolution as shown in Section 5.4. Furthermore, our architecture has a consistent error at any resolution of the inputs and outputs. On the other hand, notice that, in Figure 3, the standard CNN methods we compare against have an error that grows with the resolution.

**Quasi-linear complexity.** The weight tensor _R_ contains _k_ max _< n_ modes, so the inner multiplication has complexity _O_ ( _k_ max). Therefore, the majority of the computational cost lies in computing the Fourier transform _F_ ( _vt_ ) and its inverse. General Fourier transforms have complexity _O_ ( _n_[2] ), however, since we truncate the series the complexity is in fact _O_ ( _nk_ max), while the FFT has complexity _O_ ( _n_ log _n_ ). Generally, we have found using FFTs to be very efficient. However a uniform discretization is required.

## 5 NUMERICAL EXPERIMENTS

In this section, we compare the proposed Fourier neural operator with multiple finite-dimensional architectures as well as operator-based approximation methods on the 1-d Burgers’ equation, the 2-d Darcy Flow problem, and 2-d Navier-Stokes equation. The data generation processes are discussed in Appendices A.3.1, A.3.2, and A.3.3 respectively. We do not compare against traditional solvers (FEM/FDM) or neural-FEM type methods since our goal is to produce an efficient operator approximation that can be used for downstream applications. We demonstrate one such application to the Bayesian inverse problem in Section 5.5.

We construct our Fourier neural operator by stacking four Fourier integral operator layers as specified in (2) and (4) with the ReLU activation as well as batch normalization. Unless otherwise specified, we use _N_ = 1000 training instances and 200 testing instances. We use Adam optimizer to train for 500 epochs with an initial learning rate of 0 _._ 001 that is halved every 100 epochs. We set _k_ max _,j_ = 16 _, dv_ = 64 for the 1-d problem and _k_ max _,j_ = 12 _, dv_ = 32 for the 2-d problems. Lower resolution data are downsampled from higher resolution. All the computation is carried on a single Nvidia V100 GPU with 16GB memory.

**Remark on Resolution.** Traditional PDE solvers such as FEM and FDM approximate a single function and therefore their error to the continuum decreases as the resolution is increased. On the other hand, operator approximation is independent of the ways its data is discretized as long as all relevant information is resolved. Resolution-invariant operators have consistent error rates among different resolutions as shown in Figure 3. Further, resolution-invariant operators can do zero-shot super-resolution, as shown in Section 5.4.

Increasing it degrades their performance while FNO achieves the same errors.

**2D and 3D Convolutions.** FNO-2D, U-Net, TF-Net, and ResNet all do 2D-convolution in the spatial domain and recurrently propagate in the time domain (2D+RNN). The operator maps the solution at the previous 10 time steps to the next time step (2D functions to 2D functions). On the other hand, FNO-3D performs convolution in space-time. It maps the initial time steps directly to the full trajectory (3D functions to 3D functions). The 2D+RNN structure can propagate the solution to any arbitrary time _T_ in increments of a fixed interval length ∆ _t_ , while the Conv3D structure is fixed to the interval [0 _, T_ ] but can transfer the solution to an arbitrary time-discretization. We find the 3-d method to be more expressive and easier to train compared to its RNN-structured counterpart.

8

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## 5.4 ZERO-SHOT SUPER-RESOLUTION.

The neural operator is mesh-invariant, so it can be trained on a lower resolution and evaluated at a higher resolution, without seeing any higher resolution data (zero-shot super-resolution). Figure 1 shows an example where we train the FNO-3D model on 64 _×_ 64 _×_ 20 resolution data in the setting above with ( _ν_ = 1e _−_ 4 _, N_ = 10000) and transfer to 256 _×_ 256 _×_ 80 resolution, demonstrating super-resolution in space-time. Fourier neural operator is the only model among the benchmarks (FNO-2D, U-Net, TF-Net, and ResNet) that can do zero-shot super-resolution. And surprisingly, it can do super-resolution not only in the spatial domain but also in the temporal domain.

## 5.5 BAYESIAN INVERSE PROBLEM

In this experiment, we use a function space Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) method (Cotter et al., 2013) to draw samples from the posterior distribution of the initial vorticity in Navier-Stokes given sparse, noisy observations at time _T_ = 50. We compare the Fourier neural operator acting as a surrogate model with the traditional solvers used to generate our train-test data (both run on GPU). We generate 25,000 samples from the posterior (with a 5,000 sample burn-in period), requiring 30,000 evaluations of the forward operator.

As shown in Figure 6 (Appendix A.5), FNO and the traditional solver recover almost the same posterior mean which, when pushed forward, recovers well the late-time dynamic of Navier Stokes. In sharp contrast, FNO takes 0 _._ 005 _s_ to evaluate a single instance while the traditional solver, after being optimized to use the largest possible internal time-step which does not lead to blow-up, takes 2 _._ 2 _s_ . This amounts to 2 _._ 5 minutes for the MCMC using FNO and over 18 hours for the traditional solver. Even if we account for data generation and training time (offline steps) which take 12 hours, using FNO is still faster! Once trained, FNO can be used to quickly perform multiple MCMC runs for different initial conditions and observations, while the traditional solver will take 18 hours for every instance. Furthermore, since FNO is differentiable, it can easily be applied to PDE-constrained optimization problems without the need for the adjoint method.

**Spectral analysis.** Due to the way we parameterize _Rφ_ , the function output by (4) has at most _k_ max _,j_ Fourier modes per channel. This, however, does not mean that the Fourier neural operator can only approximate functions up to _k_ max _,j_ modes. Indeed, the activation functions which occur between integral operators and the final decoder network _Q_ recover the high frequency modes. As an example, consider a solution to the Navier-Stokes equation with viscosity _ν_ = 1e _−_ 3. Truncating this function at 20 Fourier modes yields an error around 2% while our Fourier neural operator learns the parametric dependence and produces approximations to an error of _≤_ 1% with only _k_ max _,j_ = 12 parameterized modes.

**Non-periodic boundary condition.** Traditional Fourier methods work only with periodic boundary conditions. However, the Fourier neural operator does not have this limitation. This is due to the linear transform _W_ (the bias term) which keeps the track of non-periodic boundary. As an example, the Darcy Flow and the time domain of Navier-Stokes have non-periodic boundary conditions, and the Fourier neural operator still learns the solution operator with excellent accuracy.

## 6 DISCUSSION AND CONCLUSION

**Requirements on Data.** Data-driven methods rely on the quality and quantity of data. To learn Navier-Stokes equation with Reynolds number _Re_ = 1e+4, we need to generate _N_ = 10000 training pairs _{aj, uj}_ with the numerical solver. However, for more challenging PDEs, generating a few training samples can be already very expensive. A future direction is to combine neural operators with numerical solvers to levitate the requirements on data. **Recurrent structure.** The neural operator has an iterative structure that can naturally be formulated as a recurrent network where all layers share the same parameters without sacrificing performance. (We did not impose this restriction in the experiments.) **Computer vision.** Operator learning is not restricted to PDEs. Images can naturally be viewed as real-valued functions on 2-d domains and videos simply add a temporal structure. Our approach is therefore a natural choice for problems in computer vision where invariance to discretization crucial is important (Chi et al., 2020).

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces the Fourier Neural Operator (FNO), a mesh-invariant architecture for learning operators between function spaces by parameterizing integral kernels in the Fourier domain. The authors demonstrate significant speedups and improved accuracy over traditional solvers and prior neural network-based PDE solvers across Burgers', Darcy flow, and Navier-Stokes equations.

The paper makes a compelling case for using spectral methods within deep learning architectures to achieve resolution invariance and computational efficiency. Its ability to perform zero-shot super-resolution on complex turbulent flows is a standout contribution that pushes the state-of-the-art in operator learning. The methodology is well-motivated by the limitations of finite-dimensional neural network approaches.

Overview issues:

- Lack of rigorous stability analysis for long-term integration: In Section 5.3, the authors apply the FNO to time-dependent Navier-Stokes equations. While the results show low relative error, the paper does not address the potential for error accumulation during long-term rollout. In dynamical systems, small errors in each time step can compound, leading to unphysical solutions or divergence over long durations. The authors should provide a stability analysis or at least empirical evidence of the model's behavior over significantly longer horizons than those tested, as this is a common failure mode for learned operators.

- Ambiguity regarding non-periodic boundary conditions: Section 4 and Section 19 claim that the FNO handles non-periodic boundary conditions, attributing this capability to the linear transform W. However, the core of the method relies on the spectral convolution theorem, which is inherently tied to the assumption of periodicity. The paper fails to explain how the model effectively handles non-periodic boundary constraints without significant spectral leakage or aliasing artifacts. A more detailed theoretical justification or a specific experiment comparing performance on periodic versus non-periodic domains is needed to support this claim.

- Insufficient comparison with traditional numerical solvers: The authors state in Section 13 that they do not compare against traditional solvers (FEM/FDM) because their goal is to produce an efficient surrogate. However, for a paper claiming to revolutionize scientific computing, a quantitative comparison of the FNO's accuracy against a high-fidelity traditional solver at the same computational budget would be more informative than comparing only against other neural network baselines. Without this, it is difficult to determine if the FNO's 'superior accuracy' is relative to other learning-based methods or if it truly approximates the underlying physics to a degree required for scientific research.

- Limited discussion on high-frequency mode truncation: The methodology in Section 8 relies on truncating the Fourier series at a fixed maximal number of modes k_max. While the authors argue that non-linear activation functions recover high-frequency information, there is no discussion on the limits of this recovery for systems with high Reynolds numbers where energy cascades to very small scales. The paper would be strengthened by a sensitivity analysis of the error with respect to the truncation parameter k_max, particularly for the turbulent regimes discussed in Section 5.3.

- Absence of OOD generalization analysis for PDE parameters: The paper claims to learn an entire family of PDEs, yet all experiments are conducted within a single, fixed distribution of parameters (e.g., viscosity or forcing functions). To demonstrate that the FNO truly learns the operator rather than just interpolating within the training distribution, it is necessary to evaluate the model on parameters outside the training range. I suggest performing a 'stress test' where the model is evaluated on PDEs with viscosity values or forcing configurations significantly different from those seen during training to quantify the degradation in performance.

- Lack of quantitative convergence validation for the operator: While the paper claims the FNO is a resolution-invariant operator, it lacks a formal demonstration of convergence to the true solution operator as the number of training samples or the model capacity increases. Providing a plot of the relative L2 error against the number of training samples for a simple case, such as the 1D Burgers' equation, would clarify whether the model is statistically consistent. This would help distinguish between the model's ability to learn the operator and its performance as a mere function approximator on a fixed dataset.

Recommendation: accept

Revision targets:

- Include a sensitivity analysis of the error with respect to the mode truncation parameter k_max.

- Provide a more detailed explanation or a specific experiment demonstrating how the model handles non-periodic boundary conditions without spectral leakage.

- Add a brief discussion or empirical check on the long-term stability of the rollout for the Navier-Stokes experiments.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces the Fourier Neural Operator (FNO), a novel architecture for learning solution operators of parametric PDEs by parameterizing integral kernels in Fourier space. The method demonstrates strong empirical performance on Burgers' equation, Darcy flow, and Navier-Stokes, claiming discretization invariance, zero-shot super-resolution, and significant speedups over traditional solvers. The work builds on prior neural operator literature and provides a compelling empirical foundation for spectral-based operator learning. However, several theoretical assumptions, missing generalization tests, and scaling limitations require clarification before the claims can be fully accepted.

The paper makes a solid empirical contribution by demonstrating that spectral parameterization can efficiently learn PDE solution operators across multiple benchmark problems. The architecture is well-motivated, and the experiments show clear improvements over existing graph-based and convolutional baselines. The focus on discretization invariance and zero-shot super-resolution addresses a genuine gap in deep learning for scientific computing. Nevertheless, the theoretical justification for handling non-periodic boundaries is weak, out-of-distribution generalization is untested, and the computational scaling to fully three-dimensional domains remains unverified.

Overview issues:

- FFT Periodicity Assumption vs Non-Periodic Boundary Claims: Section 5.5 asserts that the linear transform W allows FNO to handle non-periodic boundary conditions, contrasting it with traditional Fourier methods. This claim is theoretically shaky because the Fast Fourier Transform strictly enforces periodicity on the computational domain. A learned bias term can partially compensate for boundary mismatches, but it does not alter the fundamental spectral basis, which inherently wraps around domain edges. Consequently, the method may struggle with complex or irregular geometries where boundary conditions are non-trivial. The paper demonstrates this on Darcy flow with Dirichlet conditions but provides no ablation or theoretical justification for how W preserves boundary fidelity across varying resolutions. The authors should clarify the theoretical limits of this bias term, restrict the method's claims to periodic or simple Dirichlet/Neumann cases, and provide experiments on non-trivial geometries.

- Missing Out-of-Distribution Generalization Tests: The methodology in Section 2 assumes that training and test parameters are drawn identically from a measure μ. The paper entirely omits out-of-distribution generalization tests, which are critical for a method claiming to learn an entire family of PDEs. Neural operators are notoriously sensitive to distribution shifts, particularly when extrapolating to unseen Reynolds numbers, viscosity coefficients, or forcing functions. Without evaluating performance on parameters outside the training support, the model's robustness remains unverified. Readers will question whether the network merely interpolates within the training manifold or truly captures the underlying solution operator. The authors should add a dedicated evaluation section testing the model on parameter regimes or coefficient distributions not seen during training, and explicitly discuss the failure modes.

- Discretization Invariance Relies on Downsampled Training Data: Section 5.4 claims discretization invariance and demonstrates zero-shot super-resolution by training on 64^3 data and evaluating on 256^3 grids. This experiment relies on downsampling higher-resolution data for training, which means the network is implicitly exposed to the continuum limit during the data generation phase. True discretization invariance requires the architecture to produce consistent errors regardless of grid spacing, not just grid density. The paper does not test performance on non-uniform grids or varying physical cell sizes, which are common in practical finite element or finite volume simulations. Furthermore, the claim that error remains invariant across resolutions lacks a rigorous statistical analysis across multiple random seeds or parameter samples. The authors should explicitly test on non-uniform meshes and clarify whether invariance refers to grid density or actual discretization independence.

- Spectral Bias and High-Frequency Dynamics in Turbulent Flows: Section 5.5 argues that activation functions recover high-frequency modes beyond the truncated k_max Fourier modes. While mathematically plausible in principle, deep networks exhibit well-documented spectral bias, preferentially learning low-frequency components first. For turbulent Navier-Stokes flows at Re=10^4, accurately capturing energy cascade and small-scale vortices is critical. The paper reports relative L2 errors but does not analyze the spectral content of the predictions or quantify how well FNO preserves high-wavenumber dynamics compared to baselines. Without a spectral analysis or error decomposition, it is unclear whether the model smears out fine-scale features to achieve low global error. The authors should include a spectral error analysis or compare the power spectra of predicted versus ground-truth solutions to validate high-frequency fidelity.

- Memory Scaling and Computational Complexity for 3D Domains: The efficiency claims in Section 4 overlook the memory footprint of the Fourier weight tensor R_φ, which scales with k_max^d × d_v^2. For three-dimensional problems or fine spatial resolutions, storing and backpropagating through these complex-valued tensors becomes a severe bottleneck. The experiments are limited to two-dimensional spatial domains plus time, effectively avoiding the full three-dimensional spatial complexity. The paper does not discuss memory constraints or how the architecture scales to fully 3D turbulent flows, which are the primary use case for PDE solvers. The authors should provide a detailed computational complexity analysis that accounts for GPU memory limits, and include experiments or scaling laws for 3D spatial domains to substantiate the efficiency claims.

- Quantitative error scaling across resolutions for super-resolution: The paper asserts that the Fourier neural operator maintains consistent error rates across different grid resolutions and demonstrates zero-shot super-resolution with a single visual example. Readers cannot verify this claim without a systematic evaluation of how the relative L2 or H1 error changes as the output resolution increases. A table or plot reporting error metrics at multiple resolutions would confirm whether the architecture truly generalizes to finer discretizations or if performance degrades due to spectral aliasing. Adding this quantitative analysis would substantiate the resolution-invariance claim and provide practitioners with guidance on achievable accuracy at target grid sizes.

- Heuristic for selecting spectral truncation k_max: The authors fix k_max,j=12 or 16 across all experiments without explaining how to determine this hyperparameter for a new problem. In spectral methods, the number of retained Fourier modes must align with the physical scales of the solution, which vary with viscosity, Reynolds number, or forcing frequency. Without a principled rule or sensitivity analysis, users must rely on trial and error, which limits the method's practical appeal. The paper should include a discussion or experiment showing how to set k_max based on the problem's characteristic wavenumbers or energy decay rates, perhaps by analyzing the power spectrum of the ground-truth solutions.

- Ablation study isolating the Fourier layer contribution: The paper positions the Fourier neural operator as a superior alternative to standard convolutional or graph-based neural operators, yet it lacks a direct ablation study that isolates the Fourier layer from the rest of the architecture. Readers will question whether the performance gains stem from the spectral parameterization or simply from the deeper network depth and residual connections used in the experiments. A controlled comparison where the Fourier transform is replaced by a standard spatial convolution or a graph-based kernel, while keeping all other hyperparameters identical, would clarify the specific contribution of the spectral domain. Including this ablation would strengthen the architectural justification and align with standard evaluation practices for operator learning models.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add an out-of-distribution generalization evaluation section testing the model on unseen Reynolds numbers, viscosity coefficients, or forcing function distributions, and discuss failure modes.

- Clarify the theoretical limitations of the linear bias term W for non-periodic boundary conditions and provide experiments on complex or irregular geometries.

- Include a spectral error analysis comparing the power spectra of predicted versus ground-truth solutions to validate high-frequency fidelity in turbulent regimes.

- Test discretization invariance on non-uniform grids or varying physical cell sizes, rather than relying solely on downsampling from higher-resolution uniform grids.

- Provide a detailed memory and computational complexity analysis for 3D spatial domains, including scaling laws or preliminary 3D experiments to substantiate efficiency claims.

comments.title: Domain mismatch in Fourier transform definitions
comments.quote: For frequency mode $k\in D$, we have $(\mathcal{F}v_{t})(k)\in\mathbb{C}^{d_{v}}$ and $R_{\phi}(k)\in\mathbb{C}^{d_{v}\times d_{v}}$. Notice that since we assume $\kappa$ is periodic, it admits a Fourier series expansion, so we may work with the discrete modes $k\in\mathbb{Z}^{d}$.
comments.explanation: The frequency variable $k$ is incorrectly assigned to the spatial domain $D$. Since $D$ denotes the physical domain, the frequency variable must belong to the dual domain (e.g., $\mathbb{R}^d$ or $\mathbb{Z}^d$). This confusion also appears in the inverse transform definition, where the integration is written as $\int_{D}f_{j}(k)\mathrm{d}k$. Rewrite the integration domain to $\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}$ (or $\sum_{k\in\mathbb{Z}^d}$ for the discrete case) in both transform definitions. The current notation conflates physical and spectral domains, which will confuse readers tracking the mathematical derivation.

comments.title: Missing normalization in inverse FFT
comments.explanation: The inverse DFT as written lacks the standard $1/n$ normalization factor (where $n=\prod s_j$). Composing the forward and inverse transforms yields $\hat{\mathcal{F}}^{-1}(\hat{\mathcal{F}}f) = n f$, not $f$. While some deep learning frameworks absorb normalization into the forward pass or leave it implicit, defining a mathematical inverse without it is incorrect for theoretical derivations. Include a $1/n$ normalization factor before the summation to ensure the transform pair is truly invertible.

comments.title: Incorrect codomain notation for solution space
comments.quote: ution,  $G^{\dagger}:L^{\infty}((0,1)^{2};\mathbb{R}_{+})\to H_{0}^{1}((0,1)^{2};\mathbb{R}_{+})$  defined by  $a \mapsto u$ .
comments.explanation: Appending $\mathbb{R}_+$ to the Sobolev space notation is mathematically misleading. $H_0^1((0,1)^2)$ is a Hilbert space of real-valued functions vanishing on the boundary, not a cone of non-negative functions. Restricting the codomain to $\mathbb{R}_+$ incorrectly implies the solution must be non-negative, which contradicts the general forcing term $f \in L^2((0,1)^2;\mathbb{R})$. Remove the $\mathbb{R}_+$ from the codomain to correctly specify the standard Sobolev space.

comments.title: Missing uniform positivity condition for diffusion coefficient
comments.quote: with a Dirichlet boundary where  $a \in L^{\infty}((0,1)^2;\mathbb{R}_+)$  is the diffusion coefficient and  $f \in L^{2}((0,1)^{2};\mathbb{R})$  is the forcing function.
comments.explanation: The well-posedness of the elliptic boundary value problem relies on the Lax-Milgram theorem, which requires the associated bilinear form to be coercive. This coercivity fails if the diffusion coefficient $a(x)$ is allowed to vanish on a set of positive measure. The stated space $L^{\infty}((0,1)^2;\mathbb{R}_+)$ includes functions that can be zero, breaking uniqueness and continuity of the solution operator. Restrict the parameter space to coefficients bounded away from zero by adding a uniform lower bound condition $a(x) \ge a_{\min} > 0$ almost everywhere.

comments.title: Projection layers break claimed parameter invariance
comments.quote: By construction, the method shares the same learned network parameters irrespective of the discretization used on the input and output spaces.
comments.explanation: This statement overgeneralizes the resolution-invariance property. While the Fourier weight tensor is defined on a fixed set of modes and does not depend on grid size, the architecture includes input and output projection layers that operate on finite-dimensional vectors. The dimensions of these projection matrices scale with the number of grid points, so they must be resized or retrained when the discretization changes. Specify that only the spectral convolution weights are resolution-invariant, while the projection layers remain discretization-dependent.

comments.title: Overclaim of resolution-independent error
comments.quote: Furthermore, our architecture has a consistent error at any resolution of the inputs and outputs.
comments.explanation: Spectral approximations inherently balance aliasing error and truncation error, both of which depend on grid spacing and the cutoff wavenumber $k_{\text{max}}$. As resolution changes, the aliasing component shifts, meaning the total error cannot remain strictly constant across all grid sizes. Replace "consistent error at any resolution" with a more precise statement that the error remains "approximately stable" or "does not systematically degrade" across the tested resolution range. This qualification aligns with standard spectral method theory and matches the empirical results shown in the paper.

comments.title: Imprecise terminology and flawed causal claim regarding speedup
comments.quote: On the other hand, data-driven methods can directly learn the trajectory of the family of equations from the data. As a result, the learning-based method can be orders of magnitude faster than the conventional solvers.
comments.explanation: The phrase "trajectory of the family of equations" is mathematically imprecise; partial differential equations are static operators that define solution manifolds, not dynamic systems with trajectories. Additionally, the causal connector "As a result" creates a logical gap. The computational speedup of neural operators does not stem from the act of learning a mapping, but rather from replacing iterative, grid-dependent numerical solves with a fixed-cost forward pass during inference. Rewrite the sentence to explicitly link the speedup to the elimination of iterative solves during inference.

comments.title: Contradiction between stated dataset size and sample cost claim
comments.quote: To learn Navier-Stokes equation with Reynolds number $Re=1\mathrm{e}{+4}$, we need to generate $N=10000$ training pairs $\{a_{j},u_{j}\}$ with the numerical solver. However, for more challenging PDEs, generating a few training samples can be already very expensive.
comments.explanation: The passage first establishes that 10,000 training pairs are required for the high-Reynolds-number experiments, then immediately asserts that generating a few training samples is expensive. This creates a direct logical contradiction: if 10,000 pairs were generated, they constitute a large dataset, not a few, and the computational cost of producing them is substantial. The authors likely intended to emphasize that even modest dataset sizes are costly for complex PDEs, but the current wording undermines the stated experimental setup. Adjust the wording to resolve the contradiction while preserving the intended point about computational cost.

comments.title: Ambiguous mode counting in spectral analysis comparison
comments.quote: Truncating this function at $20$ Fourier modes yields an error around $2\%$ while our Fourier neural operator learns the parametric dependence and produces approximations to an error of $\leq 1\%$ with only $k_{\text{max},j}=12$ parameterized modes.
comments.explanation: The comparison between a 20-mode truncation and $k_{\text{max},j}=12$ is ambiguous because $k_{\text{max},j}$ denotes the number of retained modes per spatial dimension. In a two-dimensional domain, $k_{\text{max},j}=12$ corresponds to $12 \times 12 = 144$ total Fourier modes, not 12. Comparing 20 total modes to 144 total modes makes the FNO appear more efficient than it actually is relative to the baseline. Explicitly state the total number of modes for both the truncation baseline and the FNO to ensure a fair, transparent comparison.

comments.title: Misleading terminology for the linear operator W
comments.quote: This is due to the linear transform $W$ (the bias term) which keeps the track of non-periodic boundary.
comments.explanation: Referring to $W$ as a 'bias term' is a terminology error that conflates it with an additive constant vector. In the FNO architecture, $W$ is a learnable linear operator in physical space (typically implemented as a dense matrix multiplication or a convolution) that acts on the input field. This distinction matters because a bias term adds a fixed offset, whereas $W$ performs a linear transformation that can mix channels and spatial features. Replace 'the bias term' with 'a learnable linear operator in physical space' to accurately reflect its mathematical role.

comments.title: Generalization claim exceeds formal problem setup
comments.quote: . Operator learning is not restricted to PDEs. Images can naturally be viewed as real-valued functions on 2-d domains and videos simply add a temporal structure. Our approach is therefore a natural choice for problems in computer vision where invariance to discretization is important
comments.explanation: The formal setup explicitly restricts the target map $G^{\dagger}$ to arise as "the solution operators of parametric PDEs" and constructs the approximation theory around this specific structure. The discussion claims the method is a "natural choice" for computer vision and asserts discretization invariance, but the formal results do not establish invariance for general function spaces nor extend theoretical guarantees to non-PDE mappings. Add a clarifying sentence stating that the current theoretical guarantees apply strictly to PDE solution operators to address the gap between the formal setup and the claimed computer vision applicability.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding parameter sharing in recurrent formulations
comments.quote: The neural operator has an iterative structure that can naturally be formulated as a recurrent network where all layers share the same parameters without sacrificing performance. (We did not impose this restriction in the experiments.)
comments.explanation: The authors assert that tying parameters across layers in a recurrent formulation does not sacrifice performance, yet explicitly note that this restriction was not tested. Presenting an untested architectural variant as a factual property overstates the paper's findings and misleads readers about the model's capabilities. Parameter sharing fundamentally alters the hypothesis space and often impacts expressivity. Reframe the statement as a speculative direction or add a clarifying sentence acknowledging that this claim is preliminary and requires empirical validation.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces a novel architecture (Fourier Neural Operator) for learning PDE solution operators, achieving impressive speedups and competitive accuracy on benchmark problems. The key strengths are the elegant Fourier-domain parameterization, the demonstration of zero-shot super-resolution, and the clear empirical gains over prior operator-learning methods. However, several important limitations and methodological concerns need to be addressed before the paper can be considered fully rigorous.

Overview issues:

- Discretization invariance claim is overstated: The paper claims that the Fourier neural operator is 'discretization-invariant' and that it can learn from and evaluate functions on arbitrary discretizations. However, the architecture relies on the FFT, which requires a uniform grid. The paper acknowledges this in passing ('a uniform discretization is required') but does not discuss how this limits the claim of invariance. For non-uniform grids or irregular domains (common in real-world applications), the FFT cannot be applied directly, and the method would require interpolation or other approximations that break the claimed invariance. The paper should either qualify the claim to specify that invariance holds only for uniform grids, or discuss extensions (e.g., using non-uniform FFT) and their limitations.

- Limited evaluation on non-periodic boundary conditions: The paper states that the Fourier neural operator can handle non-periodic boundary conditions because the linear transform W 'keeps track of non-periodic boundary.' This is a strong claim that is not convincingly demonstrated. The Darcy flow example (Section 5.2) has Dirichlet boundary conditions, but the paper does not analyze how well the method performs near the boundaries compared to the interior, nor does it compare against methods designed for non-periodic domains. The claim that a simple linear transform can compensate for the periodic assumption of the Fourier basis needs rigorous justification, either through analysis or through experiments with more challenging non-periodic problems (e.g., Neumann or Robin boundary conditions).

- Spectral bias and high-frequency content are not adequately addressed: The paper acknowledges that the Fourier layer truncates high-frequency modes (k_max = 12 for 2D problems) but argues that activation functions and the decoder network recover high-frequency information. This argument is plausible but not substantiated. The spectral analysis in Section 5.5 only shows that the overall error is low; it does not demonstrate that the network actually learns high-frequency components rather than just approximating the low-frequency part well. For problems with sharp gradients or discontinuities (e.g., shocks in Burgers' equation at low viscosity), the truncation could lead to Gibbs phenomena. The paper should include a spectral analysis of the learned solution (e.g., plotting the Fourier spectrum of the prediction vs. ground truth) to show that high modes are indeed recovered, and discuss the limitations for problems with significant high-frequency content.

- Comparison with baselines is not controlled for computational budget: The paper compares FNO against several baselines (FCN, GNO, MGNO, U-Net, ResNet, TF-Net) in terms of accuracy, but does not control for the number of parameters or training time. For example, FNO-3D has 6.5M parameters while U-Net has 24.9M, yet the paper does not discuss whether the accuracy gap persists when baselines are given comparable model capacity. Similarly, the inference time comparison (0.005s vs 2.2s for the traditional solver) is impressive, but the paper should also compare inference times against learned baselines to show that FNO is not just accurate but also efficient relative to other ML methods. A fair comparison would involve tuning each baseline's capacity to a similar computational budget and reporting both accuracy and inference time.

- Generalization to unseen PDE parameters and resolutions is insufficiently tested: The paper demonstrates zero-shot super-resolution (training on 64x64, testing on 256x256) but does not systematically test generalization to other resolutions (e.g., 32x32, 128x128) or to PDE parameters outside the training range (e.g., different viscosity coefficients or forcing functions). For a method that claims to learn an operator over a family of PDEs, it is critical to show that the learned operator generalizes to unseen parameter values. The paper should include experiments where the test set contains parameters (e.g., viscosity, initial condition statistics) that are different from the training set, and report the degradation in accuracy. This is especially important for the Navier-Stokes experiments where the viscosity varies across configurations.

- Reproducibility and statistical significance are not adequately documented: The paper does not report results across multiple random seeds or data splits, making it difficult to assess the statistical significance of the reported improvements. For example, Table 1 shows single numbers for each method and configuration, but it is unclear whether the 30-60% error reduction over baselines is consistent across different initializations. The paper should report mean and standard deviation over at least 3-5 random seeds, and specify how the training/validation/test split was performed. Additionally, the paper mentions that code and data generation scripts are available, but the main text does not provide a link or a clear statement about reproducibility. These are standard expectations for top-tier ML venues.

- No demonstration on irregular or non-uniform grids: The paper claims discretization invariance but the architecture relies on the FFT, which requires a uniform grid. The acknowledgment that 'a uniform discretization is required' is buried in the text and not tested. The paper should include an experiment where the trained FNO is evaluated on a non-uniform grid (e.g., by interpolating the output to irregular points) and show that the error remains low, or explicitly qualify the claim to specify that invariance holds only for uniform grids. Without this, the central claim of resolution invariance is misleading.

- Missing systematic generalization to unseen PDE parameters: The paper tests zero-shot super-resolution but does not evaluate generalization to PDE parameters outside the training range, such as different viscosity coefficients or forcing functions. For a method that claims to learn an operator over a family of PDEs, it is critical to show that the learned operator generalizes to unseen parameter values. The paper should include experiments where the test set contains parameters (e.g., viscosity, initial condition statistics) that are different from the training set, and report the degradation in accuracy. This is especially important for the Navier-Stokes experiments where the viscosity varies across configurations.

- No spectral analysis of learned high-frequency recovery: The paper argues that activation functions and the decoder recover high-frequency modes truncated by the Fourier layer, but provides no spectral analysis to support this. The spectral analysis in Section 5.5 only shows overall error, not the spectrum of the prediction vs. ground truth. The paper should include plots of the Fourier spectrum of the learned solution for a representative test case (e.g., Navier-Stokes at Re=1e-4) to demonstrate that high modes are indeed recovered, and discuss limitations for problems with sharp gradients or discontinuities.

- Missing comparison of inference time with learned baselines: The paper compares inference time against a traditional solver (0.005s vs 2.2s) but does not compare inference times against learned baselines like U-Net, ResNet, or TF-Net. Since the paper claims speed advantages, it should report inference times for all methods under the same hardware and resolution. This would show whether FNO is not only more accurate but also faster than other ML approaches, which is important for practical deployment.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Qualify the discretization invariance claim to specify that it holds for uniform grids, and discuss limitations for non-uniform or irregular domains.

- Provide a rigorous analysis or additional experiments demonstrating that the method handles non-periodic boundary conditions effectively, including quantitative comparison near boundaries.

- Include a spectral analysis (e.g., Fourier spectrum of predictions vs. ground truth) to support the claim that high-frequency modes are recovered despite truncation, and discuss limitations for problems with discontinuities.

- Add controlled comparisons with baselines where model capacity (parameters) or training time is matched, and report inference times for all learned methods.

- Report results with statistical significance (mean and std over multiple seeds) and provide a clear reproducibility statement with a link to code and data.

comments.title: Spectral analysis does not support high-frequency recovery claim
comments.quote: Truncating this function at $20$ Fourier modes yields an error around $2\%$ while our Fourier neural operator learns the parametric dependence and produces approximations to an error of $\leq 1\%$ with only $k_{\text{max},j}=12$ parameterized modes.
comments.explanation: The comparison does not show that high-frequency modes are recovered. The FNO's lower error with fewer modes could simply mean the learned 12-mode representation is better optimized than a naive truncation at 20 modes, not that modes beyond 12 are accurately reproduced. To substantiate the claim, the paper should compare the Fourier spectra of the FNO prediction and the ground truth, showing that high-frequency content (beyond 12 modes) is present in the FNO output.

comments.title: Non-periodic boundary claim lacks theoretical or experimental support
comments.quote: This is due to the linear transform $W$ (the bias term) which keeps the track of non-periodic boundary. As an example, the Darcy Flow and the time domain of Navier-Stokes have non-periodic boundary conditions, and the Fourier neural operator still learns the solution operator with excellent accuracy.
comments.explanation: The paper provides no analysis or experiment to demonstrate that the linear transform W compensates for the periodic assumption of the Fourier basis. The Darcy flow example has Dirichlet boundary conditions, but the paper does not show that the error near boundaries is comparable to the interior, nor does it compare against methods designed for non-periodic domains. A single linear transform per Fourier layer is unlikely to fully capture spatially localized and potentially nonlinear boundary effects. The claim would require either a theoretical argument or a careful numerical experiment comparing boundary vs. interior error.

comments.title: Discretization invariance claim contradicts FFT requirement
comments.quote: The Fourier layers are discretization-invariant because they can learn from and evaluate functions which are discretized in an arbitrary way.
comments.explanation: The FFT used in the architecture requires a uniform grid, so 'arbitrary' discretizations (e.g., irregular or adaptive meshes) are not directly supported. The paper itself notes in Section 4 that 'a uniform discretization is required.' The projection onto Fourier basis functions is indeed well-defined continuously, but the discrete Fourier transform via FFT assumes equally spaced samples. For non-uniform discretizations, interpolation or non-uniform FFT would be needed, which the paper does not discuss. The claim should be qualified to specify invariance to uniform grid refinements.

comments.title: Target space regularity claim for Burgers' equation is unjustified
comments.quote: We aim to learn the operator mapping the initial condition to the solution at time one,  $G^\dagger: L_{\mathrm{per}}^2((0,1); \mathbb{R}) \to H_{\mathrm{per}}^r((0,1); \mathbb{R})$  defined by  $u_0 \mapsto u(\cdot, 1)$  for any  $r > 0$ .
comments.explanation: The claim that the operator maps into $H_{\mathrm{per}}^r$ for any $r>0$ is not supported by the assumptions. For Burgers' equation with $u_0 \in L^2$ and periodic boundary conditions, the solution at time one is not guaranteed to be in $H^r$ for arbitrary $r>0$, especially for small viscosity $\nu$ where shocks may form. Standard regularity theory typically yields $u(\cdot,1) \in H^1$ if $u_0 \in L^2$, but not $H^r$ for all $r>0$. Rewrite the target space as $L_{\mathrm{per}}^2((0,1); \mathbb{R})$ or specify a regularity that can be guaranteed under the given assumptions.

comments.title: Operator domain for Navier-Stokes excludes zero initial time
comments.quote: $G^{\dagger}:C([0,10];H_{\mathrm{per}}^{r}((0,1)^{2};\mathbb{R})) \to C((10,T];H_{\mathrm{per}}^{r}((0,1)^{2};\mathbb{R}))$  defined by  $w|_{(0,1)^2\times [0,10]}\mapsto w|_{(0,1)^2\times (10,T]}$ .
comments.explanation: The operator domain is $C([0,10];H_{\mathrm{per}}^{r}((0,1)^{2};\mathbb{R}))$, which requires the vorticity to be continuous in time on $[0,10]$. However, the initial condition $w_0$ is only in $L_{\mathrm{per}}^2$, not necessarily continuous. The solution $w$ may not be continuous at $t=0$ if $w_0$ is not continuous. The domain should be $C((0,10];H_{\mathrm{per}}^{r})$ or include a separate condition for $t=0$.

comments.title: Missing statistical significance and seed variability
comments.quote: Unless otherwise specified, we use $N=1000$ training instances and $200$ testing instances. We use Adam optimizer to train for $500$ epochs with an initial learning rate of $0.001$ that is halved every $100$ epochs.
comments.explanation: The section describes the training setup but does not report results over multiple random seeds or data splits. Without this, the reader cannot assess whether the reported error reductions (30–60% over baselines) are statistically significant. The paper should report mean and standard deviation over at least 3–5 random initializations and specify how the train/test split was performed.

comments.title: No comparison of inference time with learned baselines
comments.quote: We do not compare against traditional solvers (FEM/FDM) or neural-FEM type methods since our goal is to produce an efficient operator approximation that can be used for downstream applications.
comments.explanation: The paper later compares inference time against a traditional solver (0.005s vs 2.2s) but does not compare inference times against learned baselines like U-Net, ResNet, or TF-Net. Since the paper claims speed advantages, it should report inference times for all methods under the same hardware and resolution. This would show whether FNO is not only more accurate but also faster than other ML approaches.

comments.title: Missing ablation on number of Fourier modes and network depth
comments.quote: We set $k_{\text{max},j}=16,d_{v}=64$ for the 1-d problem and $k_{\text{max},j}=12,d_{v}=32$ for the 2-d problems.
comments.explanation: The choice of $k_{\text{max},j}$ and $d_v$ is given without justification or ablation. The paper claims that truncating high-frequency modes is compensated by activation functions and the decoder, but provides no experiment varying $k_{\text{max},j}$ to show the trade-off between accuracy and computational cost. An ablation study on these hyperparameters is necessary to justify the design choices and understand their impact on performance.

comments.title: No demonstration of generalization to unseen PDE parameters
comments.quote: We construct our Fourier neural operator by stacking four Fourier integral operator layers as specified in (2) and (4) with the ReLU activation as well as batch normalization.
comments.explanation: The section describes the architecture but does not test generalization to PDE parameters outside the training range (e.g., different viscosity coefficients or forcing functions). For a method that claims to learn an operator over a family of PDEs, it is critical to show that the learned operator generalizes to unseen parameter values. The paper should include experiments where the test set contains parameters different from the training set and report the degradation in accuracy.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about high-frequency recovery in contributions
comments.quote: We observed that the proposed framework can approximate complex operators raising in PDEs that are highly non-linear, with high frequency modes and slow energy decay.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that the Fourier neural operator can handle 'high frequency modes and slow energy decay,' but the architecture truncates Fourier modes at k_max=12. The paper provides no spectral analysis (e.g., comparing the Fourier spectrum of predictions vs. ground truth) to support this claim. The authors should either provide such analysis or qualify the claim.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about downstream application accuracy
comments.quote: Despite its tremendous speed advantage, the method does not suffer from accuracy degradation when used in downstream applications such as solving the Bayesian inverse problem, as shown in Figure 6.
comments.explanation: The claim that the method 'does not suffer from accuracy degradation' in downstream applications is based on a single figure (Figure 6). The paper does not provide quantitative metrics (e.g., relative error, posterior coverage) or compare against a baseline solver to demonstrate that accuracy is preserved. The authors should either provide quantitative results or soften the claim.

comments.title: MCMC convergence not verified for surrogate model
comments.quote: We generate 25,000 samples from the posterior (with a 5,000 sample burn-in period), requiring 30,000 evaluations of the forward operator.
comments.explanation: The paper does not verify that the MCMC chain using the FNO surrogate has converged to the same posterior as the chain using the traditional solver. The comparison of posterior means (Figure 6) is a necessary but not sufficient check; the paper should also compare posterior variances or credible intervals. Without such verification, the claim that FNO 'recovers almost the same posterior mean' may be coincidental or may hide systematic bias in the surrogate.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about recurrent structure
comments.quote: The neural operator has an iterative structure that can naturally be formulated as a recurrent network where all layers share the same parameters without sacrificing performance. (We did not impose this restriction in the experiments.)
comments.explanation: The claim that sharing parameters across layers does not sacrifice performance is not supported by any experiment or analysis in the paper. The parenthetical note that the authors did not impose this restriction in their experiments further undermines the claim, as it suggests the claim is speculative rather than empirically validated. The authors should either provide experimental evidence or rephrase the statement as a conjecture or future direction.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about computer vision applications
comments.quote: Operator learning is not restricted to PDEs. Images can naturally be viewed as real-valued functions on 2-d domains and videos simply add a temporal structure. Our approach is therefore a natural choice for problems in computer vision where invariance to discretization crucial is important *(Chi et al., 2020)*.
comments.explanation: The claim that the Fourier neural operator is a 'natural choice' for computer vision problems is not supported by any experiments or analysis in the paper. The reference to Chi et al. (2020) is not discussed, and the paper does not demonstrate that the FNO outperforms or is competitive with established computer vision architectures on any vision task. The authors should either remove this speculative claim or provide a concrete example or experiment showing the FNO's effectiveness on a vision task.

comments.title: Inconsistent benchmark labeling for Burgers equation
comments.quote: Left: benchmarks on Burgers equation; Mid: benchmarks on Darcy Flow for different resolutions; Right: the learning curves on Navier-Stokes  $\nu = 1\mathrm{e} - 3$  with different benchmarks. Train and test on the same resolution. For acronyms, see Section 5; details in Tables 1, 3, 4.
comments.explanation: The caption states 'Train and test on the same resolution' for all three panels, but the Darcy Flow benchmark (mid panel) is described as 'for different resolutions' in the same sentence. This is contradictory: if training and testing are on the same resolution, then the Darcy panel cannot be testing different resolutions. Rewrite the caption to clearly separate the resolution-invariance experiment from the same-resolution benchmark.

comments.title: Abstract overstates zero-shot super-resolution claim
comments.quote: The Fourier neural operator is the first ML-based method to successfully model turbulent flows with zero-shot super-resolution.
comments.explanation: The claim that FNO is the 'first ML-based method to successfully model turbulent flows with zero-shot super-resolution' is not supported by the evidence presented in the paper. The experiments on Navier-Stokes (Section 5.3) train on 64×64 resolution and test on 256×256, but the paper does not compare against any baseline that also attempts zero-shot super-resolution for turbulent flows. Without such a comparison, the claim of being 'first' is unsubstantiated. The authors should either provide a comparison with a relevant baseline or remove the word 'first'.

comments.title: Abstract's accuracy claim lacks statistical significance
comments.quote: Additionally, it achieves superior accuracy compared to previous learning-based solvers under fixed resolution.
comments.explanation: The claim of 'superior accuracy' is based on single numbers reported in Table 1, without any measure of variance (e.g., standard deviation over multiple random seeds). In machine learning, performance can vary significantly across initializations, and the paper does not provide any statistical significance test. The authors should report mean and standard deviation over at least 3-5 random seeds for all methods and all benchmarks.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of traditional solvers in introduction
comments.quote: As a consequence, traditional numerical solvers are slow and sometimes inefficient.
comments.explanation: This statement is too broad and potentially misleading. Traditional numerical solvers can be highly efficient for many problems, especially when adaptive meshing or fast solvers (like multigrid) are used. The paper's own experiments show that a traditional solver takes 2.2 seconds for a single Navier-Stokes solve, which is not 'slow' in absolute terms—it is only slow relative to the 0.005 seconds of FNO. The claim should be qualified to reflect that the slowness is relative to the need for many evaluations.

comments.title: Overly broad claim about data-driven methods in introduction
comments.quote: On the other hand, data-driven methods can directly learn the trajectory of the family of equations from the data. As a result, the learning-based method can be orders of magnitude faster than the conventional solvers.
comments.explanation: The claim that 'data-driven methods can directly learn the trajectory of the family of equations from the data' is imprecise. Data-driven methods learn an approximation of the solution operator from paired input-output examples; they do not 'learn the trajectory' in the sense of simulating the PDE dynamics. Moreover, the statement that 'the learning-based method can be orders of magnitude faster than the conventional solvers' is presented as a general fact without qualification. The text should be rewritten to clarify that the speed advantage applies to the proposed method after training.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of classical neural networks in introduction
comments.quote: However, classical neural networks map between finite-dimensional spaces and can therefore only learn solutions tied to a specific discretization.
comments.explanation: This statement conflates two distinct concepts: the finite-dimensional nature of standard neural network mappings and the ability to generalize across discretizations. While it is true that a standard fully-connected or convolutional network takes a fixed-size input and produces a fixed-size output, this does not inherently prevent learning solutions that can be evaluated at arbitrary points. For example, a network can be trained to map sensor measurements to a latent representation that is then decoded at query points, as done in DeepONet or PINNs. The text should be revised to avoid implying that any finite-dimensional network is inherently tied to a specific discretization.

comments.title: Misleading claim about resolution invariance in introduction
comments.quote: In contrast, we show, for our method, both invariance of the error to grid resolution, and the ability to transfer the solution between meshes.
comments.explanation: The paper claims 'invariance of the error to grid resolution' but the architecture relies on the FFT, which requires a uniform grid. The paper acknowledges this limitation only in passing later ('a uniform discretization is required'), but the claim here is unqualified and misleading. The authors should rewrite this sentence to specify that invariance holds only for uniform grids.

comments.title: Overstatement about zero-shot super-resolution in contributions
comments.quote: It can do zero-shot super-resolution: trained on a lower resolution directly evaluated on a higher resolution, as shown in Figure 1.
comments.explanation: The claim of 'zero-shot super-resolution' is supported only by a single example (Navier-Stokes, 64x64 to 256x256). The paper does not test generalization to other resolutions (e.g., 32x32, 128x128) or to different PDE parameters. Without systematic evaluation, this claim is overstated. The authors should either provide additional experiments or qualify the claim to reflect the limited evidence.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about equal errors at higher resolutions for Navier-Stokes
comments.quote: Note that we only present results for spatial resolution  $64 \times 64$  since all benchmarks we compare against are designed for this resolution. Increasing it degrades their performance while FNO achieves the same errors.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that increasing resolution degrades baseline performance while FNO maintains the same errors, but no quantitative results are provided for the Navier-Stokes case. The zero-shot super-resolution experiment in Section 5.4 shows only a qualitative example without reporting numerical errors at higher resolutions. Without such evidence, the claim is unsubstantiated. Add a table or figure reporting relative L2 errors at multiple resolutions for the Navier-Stokes equation to support this claim.

comments.title: Operator codomain interval notation for Navier-Stokes
comments.quote: $G^{\dagger}:C([0,10];H_{\mathrm{per}}^{r}((0,1)^{2};\mathbb{R})) \to C((10,T];H_{\mathrm{per}}^{r}((0,1)^{2};\mathbb{R}))$  defined by  $w|_{(0,1)^2\times [0,10]}\mapsto w|_{(0,1)^2\times (10,T]}$ .
comments.explanation: The codomain uses the half-open interval $(10,T]$, but the solution $w$ is continuous on $[0,T]$ by the PDE system (8). The restriction $w|_{(0,1)^2\times (10,T]}$ is therefore continuous on the closed interval $[10,T]$, so the codomain should be $C([10,T];H_{\mathrm{per}}^{r}((0,1)^{2};\mathbb{R}))$. This is a minor notational inconsistency.

comments.title: MCMC timing comparison should clarify amortization
comments.quote: Even if we account for data generation and training time (offline steps) which take $12$ hours, using FNO is still faster! Once trained, FNO can be used to quickly perform multiple MCMC runs for different initial conditions and observations, while the traditional solver will take $18$ hours for every instance.
comments.explanation: The comparison is asymmetric: the 12-hour offline cost for FNO includes data generation and training, but the traditional solver's '18 hours for every instance' does not include the cost of generating the training data (which is the same data used to train FNO). For a single MCMC run, FNO is indeed faster (12 hours + 2.5 minutes vs. 18 hours). However, the claim 'still faster' is correct only if the offline cost is amortized over multiple runs. The text should clarify this amortization argument explicitly to avoid misleading readers.

comments.title: Overstated claim about data generation cost in conclusion
comments.quote: To learn Navier-Stokes equation with Reynolds number $Re=1\mathrm{e}{+4}$, we need to generate $N=10000$ training pairs $\{a_{j},u_{j}\}$ with the numerical solver. However, for more challenging PDEs, generating a few training samples can be already very expensive.
comments.explanation: The statement that 'generating a few training samples can be already very expensive' is vague and potentially misleading. The paper does not specify what constitutes 'a few' or quantify the expense relative to the 10,000 samples used for the Navier-Stokes example. Without concrete examples or a discussion of the computational cost of generating training data for more challenging PDEs, this claim lacks support. The authors should either provide a specific example or remove the sentence.
